title: Like I'm Five
alt: 'Am I taking care of you? I have a thesis to write!' 'My parents are at their house; you visited last--' 'No, no, explain like you're five.'
transcript:
Cueball: What've you been up to?
Megan: Doing tons of math for my thesis.
Cueball: Can you explain it like I'm five?
Megan: "Oh my god, where are your parents?"
explanation:
Megan tells Cueball that she is working on her math . A thesis consists of original research and generally deals with material that is difficult for the uninitiated to understand. Cueball anticipates that it will be difficult for him to understand, and asks her to "explain it like I'm five". "Explain it like I'm five" is a way of asking for a simpler explanation of some difficult topic, in a way that a five-year-old child would be able to understand. Megan misinterprets this and treats Cueball as if he were an actual 5-year-old without his parents, expressing her concern of seeing a 5-year-old without supervision. This is an example of idiomatic language being taken literally, something that Randall has explored in other comics as well, such as 168: Reverse Euphemisms, 1261: Shake That, and 1454: Done. 

The common expression "Explain it like I'm five" is parodied by a line by  in his movie . "Why a four-year-old child could understand this report! Run out and find me a four-year-old child, I can't make head or tail of it."

In the title text, Megan feigns concern that she will have to abandon her work to take care of this supposed lost child and takes this role-playing further by refusing to respond to Cueball until he rephrases his comments as a 5-year-old would.
